title: Reducing code review queue time without increasing low-quality merges
summary: Cut median wait time by scaling what already works (rotation + automation for routine changes) while explicitly targeting tail latency as an ownership-routing problem. Use automation to organize and route work, not to bypass approvals, and apply fast-track only where policy and defect data indicate low risk (docs/config with healthy tests).

claims.text: Median review wait time dropped from 19h to 13h after adding reviewer rotation, but 90th percentile wait remains 46h because complex changes bottleneck on the same two senior reviewers.
Tail latency is driven mainly by cross-service/shared-service changes waiting on scarce, specific owners; docs and config changes moved quickly after rotation.

Reviewers are positive about auto-assignment for routine changes but distrust bot approvals when tests are flaky or ownership is unclear; they want explainable risk labels (ownership metadata, test health, file categories) and the ability to challenge a fast-track label before merge.

Post-merge data shows no measurable defect increase for low-risk docs/config changes, but incidents occurred when cross-service changes were fast-tracked without the owning team; leadership wants different treatment for low-risk vs cross-boundary changes and a distinction between queue organization and approval authority.
Proposed tooling can suggest reviewers, group low-risk changes, and surface missing ownership metadata, but cannot verify semantic ownership and cannot override branch protection.

openQuestions: Which shared services or paths account for most of the 46h p90 tail, and which two senior reviewers are the recurring bottleneck (by repo/path)?
What objective criteria define 'healthy tests' in practice (e.g., recent flake rate threshold) to avoid reviewer distrust of fast-track?
Where are ownership entries most stale/conflicting in shared services, and who owns fixing them (team/process)?
How will 'challenge before merge' work operationally (time window, who can challenge, what happens when challenged)?
recommendation:
1) Target tail latency via ownership routing, not more blanket staffing: introduce an explicit cross-service lane that requires owning-team review and routes directly to the correct owning team, rather than repeatedly queueing behind two senior reviewers. This aligns with the finding that p90 is dominated by complex cross-service work and should be treated as an ownership problem. [S1][S3]

2) Expand fast-track only for low-risk docs/config changes with healthy tests, and keep it strictly as queue prioritization—not approval bypass. This is supported by defect data and policy allowance, while avoiding the documented cross-service fast-track failure mode. [S3][S6]

3) Implement explainable risk labeling to increase trust: for every suggested fast-track, show why it’s low risk (file categories, ownership confidence, test health) and show uncertainty (missing/conflicting ownership) as a risk signal rather than hiding it behind auto-assignment. Pair with a “challenge before merge” mechanism so reviewers can block questionable fast-tracks. [S2][S5][S6]

4) Use the queue helper for organization and routing: cluster similar low-risk changes, suggest reviewers for routine work, and prominently flag missing/conflicting ownership metadata. Do not attempt bot approvals, since reviewers distrust that and tooling can’t verify semantic ownership anyway. [S2][S4]

5) Fix ownership metadata in shared services as a throughput lever: prioritize correcting stale/inherited ownership entries in shared services since that’s where waits concentrate; make missing/conflicting ownership visible and actionable to prevent mis-routing and avoid cross-service incidents. [S1][S5][S3]
